Economists study how people make decisions: how much they work, what they buy, how much they save, and how they invest savings. Economists also study how people interact with one another. For example, they examine how buyers and sellers interact in markets to determine prices at which goods and services are sold. Market economy allocates resources through decentralized decisions of many firms and households as they interact in markets for goods and services. Each household and each firm acts as if led by invisible hand that guides them to desirable market outcomes. Prices are instrument with which invisible hand directs economic activity. Prices reflect both value of good to society and cost to society of making good. When government prevents prices from adjusting naturally to supply and demand, it impedes invisible hand ability to coordinate decisions of households and firms that make up economy. Economists are understanding that sometimes government intervention is necessary to promote efficiency or equity. For instance, when market fails to allocate resources efficiently, government can intervene to improve market outcome. But even when government policies are designed to promote equity, they can reduce efficiency. Me and my colleagues are usually going to university for to teach students about principles of economics. Sometimes, students are asking us questions about how market works. We are explaining that incentives are playing big role in economics. If you want to understand economics, you must look at how people respond to incentives. For example, when price of good rises, buyers are buying less and sellers are selling more. This is principle which economists are calling it law of demand and supply. You get the point? If you are wanting to know more, you can read textbook by N. Gregory Mankiw.
